zotero.abstractNote: Aims: Risk stratification in patients with acute coronary syndromes (ACS) is essential for clinical decision-making. We aimed to improve 1-year mortality prediction in ACS patients by applying machine learning (ML) to a comprehensive set of clinical and laboratory variables. Methods and results: We included 1945 patients with ACS from the SPUM-ACS cohort. We compared the performance of a random forest (RF) model with the Global Registry of Acute Coronary Events (GRACE) score for predicting 1-year all-cause mortality. The RF model was trained on 128 clinical and laboratory variables, including novel biomarkers. The primary outcome was 1-year all-cause mortality. The RF model achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.86 (95% CI 0.81–0.91), which was significantly higher than the GRACE score (AUC 0.78, 95% CI 0.72–0.84, P 0.001). The RF model also showed superior calibration and clinical utility in decision curve analysis. The most important predictors were age, N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide, and high-sensitivity cardiac troponin T. Conclusion: Machine learning significantly improves 1-year mortality prediction in ACS patients compared with the established GRACE score. Our findings suggest that integrating a broad range of clinical and laboratory data into ML models can enhance risk stratification in ACS patients.
